text: Hello and welcome back to Wired Workshop! In our last session, we broke down the physical components of a network: the end devices, the intermediary devices, and the media that connects them all. We learned to identify the destinations, the intersections, and the pavement of our digital city.

But just knowing the pieces isn't enough. How do network engineers keep track of dozens, hundreds, or even thousands of devices? How do they plan for growth or troubleshoot a problem when something goes down? They don't just keep it all in their heads. Instead, they use maps.

Today, we're diving into the world of network representations and topologies. We’re going to learn how to read the language of network diagrams, understand the icons that represent complex devices, and discover the two main types of maps—or topologies—that professionals use to visualize and manage networks. By the end of this video, you'll be able to look at a professional network diagram and understand exactly what you're seeing. Let's get started.

Network Representations: The Language of Diagrams — When it comes to documenting a network, a picture is truly worth a thousand words. Network engineers use topology diagrams to create a visual map of the network. These diagrams use standardized symbols and icons to represent devices and connections. Learning these symbols is like learning the alphabet of networking.

End Devices — These are the devices we use every day. In a diagram, you'll see icons for a desktop computer, a laptop, a printer, or an IP phone. Servers are often depicted as a rectangular box, the workhorses of the network.

Intermediary Devices — These direct traffic. A wireless router might appear as a small box with antennas. A LAN Switch is a rectangle with multiple arrows. The Router, which connects networks, is almost always a circle with an X-like set of arrows.

Network Media — Connections are just as important. A wireless link is shown as a jagged or lightning-bolt line. A LAN connection, like Ethernet, is a straight black line. A WAN link, connecting to the outside world, is often red or jagged.
Topology Diagrams: The Blueprints of a Network — A topology shows how the pieces are arranged and connected. In networking we use two kinds of maps: Physical Topologies and Logical Topologies.

Physical Topology — Shows the actual physical location of devices and how cables are run. Think of an architect's blueprint: which room a server is in, which switch ports are used, and how cables route through walls and ceilings. It answers: Where is everything located?

Logical Topology — Abstracts away physical placement and focuses on how data flows. It emphasizes IP addresses, subnets, VLANs, and routing. Like a subway map: it shows stations and connections, not the exact twists of tunnels. It answers: How is everything addressed and connected?
Subway Map Analogy — A city map shows every road and building. A subway map shows only stations and lines—just enough to navigate. Logical topologies do the same for data flow.
Why both matter — Use the physical diagram to locate a bad cable or port. Use the logical diagram to track an addressing issue or a routing problem. Together, they provide a complete picture.
Putting it together — Imagine a simple office network.
Physical example — The server is in a closet, a switch is rack-mounted, and Ethernet cables run through the walls to jacks in each office where desktops are plugged in.

Logical example — The map shows the server IP, the client IP range, any VLANs, and the edge router that connects the office to the internet. Devices are part of the same logical group even if they’re in different rooms.

Recap — Professionals use standardized icons to represent end devices, intermediary devices, and media. Physical topology shows where devices are and how they’re wired. Logical topology shows how data flows and how the network is addressed.
You now have the skills to read professional topology diagrams and understand the story they tell. This is a crucial skill for anyone serious about networking.
Thanks for joining me at Wired Workshop. If this lesson helped you decode network diagrams, hit like and subscribe for more CCNA lessons. Questions? Drop them below. Until next time—keep learning!
